taskVersion.name: epu_q2_short
taskVersion.definition:
@kbench.task(name="epu_q2_short")
def epu_q2_short(
    llm,
    article_key: str,
    content_for_eval: str,
    gold_epu: int,
    short_pre: str = "",
    short_post: str = "",
    medium_pre: str = "",
    medium_post: str = "",
    long_pre: str = "",
    long_post: str = "",
    disagreement_flag: int = 0,
) -> float:
    out = model_json_call(llm, build_q2_prompt(content_for_eval, short_pre, short_post))
    m = binary_match(out["epu"], gold_epu)
    return 0.0 if m is None else float(m)

parts.text:
You are evaluating only the text between TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END.

Task:
Decide whether the TARGET ARTICLE expresses ECONOMIC POLICY UNCERTAINTY (EPU).

Labeling rule:
- EPU = 1 only if the TARGET ARTICLE discusses uncertainty about government policy,
  policy decisions, possible policy actions, political outcomes affecting policy,
  or the economic effects of policy.
- EPU = 0 for general economics, elections, markets, war, uncertainty, or foreign events
  unless the uncertainty is specifically tied to economic policy.

IMPORTANT:
- Use only the text inside TARGET ARTICLE START and TARGET ARTICLE END for the decision.
- Return ONLY valid JSON.
- No markdown. No explanation. No code fence.

Return exactly these keys:
{
  "epu": 0 or 1,
  "confidence": number between 0 and 1,
  "focus_excerpt": "short quote from target article only"
}

Archi

TARGET ARTICLE START
Abstract: Florida Power & Light hires new crew of trained -- and well-paid -- workers for Turkey Point from a special Miami Dade College program at the Homestead campus. BY TANIA VALDEMORO tvaldemoro@MiamiHerald.com The last place Gabriel Aldana, a student at the Homestead campus of Miami Dade College, expected to work was at Florida Power & Light's Turkey Point nuclear power station. Two years ago, he was studying law and unsure of his future. He picked up information about the college's new two-year electrical power technology program and decided to enroll. Thursday, Aldana, 23, of Homestead, sttod with his classmates at a new-hire ceremony, where FPL officials formally presented him with a job offer. "I'm fortunate, blessed and excited," Aldana said. At a time when fewer graduates are finding work after completing their degrees and many people are uncertain they will keep the jobs they have, 17 graduates of MDC's electrical power technology program are leaving the schtol with a well-paying job -- and opportunities for advancement. The salaries are $45,000 and higher, said Sarah Marmion, an FPL spokeswoman. The newly minted graduates will be part of Turkey Point's maintenance crew -- working in one of three disciplines: mechanical, electrical, and instrumentation and controls. Aldana will be part of the mechanical unit. "I'll be working with pumps and valves, rigging and lifting the main head, which has nuclear material," he said. The new-hire ceremony is a point of pride f

's Homestead campus. "We have been able to grow and retain our own local talent and support economic development in our community," she said. FPL and MDC are expanding their partnership in the fall. They will launch another two-year training program where students can learn the operations discipline and possibly get jobs in that division of the Turkey Point nuclear plant after graduation, said James Auld, FPL's college ctordinator. For Esperanza Lapaix, 31, her new job at FPL offers her peace of mind. "I'm excited, knowing that I'll be working at a stable job," Lapaix said. She had been working at the college's network and computer technology department when she heard about the program. Lapaix will work in the instrumentation and controls unit. She and the other new workers will have to wait a few weeks to get their security clearance before starting their new jobs. Jefferson told the graduates, "Your learning has not stopped once you leave Miami Dade College. Take your job seriously. Learn your craft. Ask questions." He added: "I ltok forward to seeing you at the plant." Caption: PHOTOS BY ALLISON DIAZ/FOR THE MIAMI HERALD GRADisATING: Above, from left, graduating students Gabriel Aldana and Carlos Marin talk before the ceremony. Above right, from left, Ying Song, faculty member, talks with graduating student Esperanza Lapaix. Below, Dr. Richard White, director of the Schtol of Computer and Engineering Technologies, speaks during the ceremony.
TARGET ARTICLE END
